acceptance_targets: [CH10_CRAFT_HEADCOUNT_CONTINUITY] A reader tracking the revised chapter can derive one consistent count: nineteen living people before Jun dies, twelve total bodies after Jun in the aftermath beat anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:143, and thirteen after Oleg in the end-state anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:221. Jun is alive in the third-floor scene before dying once on page; if any line still implies he died before the fight or that the cafeteria holds more living people than exist, this fails.

[CH10_CRAFT_TOMAS_CAUSALITY] By the end of the revised Tomás/Oleg sequence anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:131 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:171, a cold reader can answer from chapter text alone what new information Tomás received, what he inferred about Ember using him, and why Oleg is the victim or witness in this beat. If those links still require the context pack or off-page knowledge, this fails.

[CH10_PROSE_TOMAS_POV_OVERREACH] In the revised bathroom scene anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:191 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:207, the prose never states unobservable Tomás intent as fact unless the scene provides direct evidence on page. A reader should be able to point to Noor's interpretive frame rather than narrator omniscience; if the text still asserts what Tomás wants or plans without evidence, this fails.

[CH10_CRAFT_OVERSTATEMENT_AFTER_ARRIVAL] In the revised cafeteria span anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:57 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:69, once Viktor takes the tray the beat ends on a new observed consequence rather than another abstract statement that his authority has collapsed. In the revised Jun/aftermath span anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:103 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:149, the chapter gives one analysis or note-entry about self-sustaining violence, not multiple sentences restating the same thesis.
[F17] No chapter contains two deaths that serve the same narrative function (demonstrating the same dynamic) unless the second death escalates or inverts the pattern of the first.
[F23] No narration passage contains more than one consecutive sentence fragment (grammatically incomplete sentence) outside of dialogue.
[R003] Ch10 uses 'chaired,' 'panels,' 'committees,' 'keynote,' and 'tenure' collectively no more than once.
[R012] Ch10 line 2020 does not list three or more fidgeting props.

findings.acceptance_test: A reader tracking the revised chapter can derive one consistent count: nineteen living people before Jun dies, twelve total bodies after Jun in the aftermath beat anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:143, and thirteen after Oleg in the end-state anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:221. Jun is alive in the third-floor scene before dying once on page; if any line still implies he died before the fight or that the cafeteria holds more living people than exist, this fails.
findings.problem: The chapter's factual floor breaks twice. It counts sixteen people already in the cafeteria before Viktor enters with six more even though only nineteen people are alive, and it says Jun died yesterday before staging Jun's Day 9 fight and death. Because this chapter's power shift depends on who is present and how many bodies are in the building, the contradictory math and pre-killing of Jun read as outright continuity failure rather than harmless imprecision.
findings.rewrite_direction: Re-audit every live/dead count and room-occupancy reference at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:5; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:9; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:11; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:27; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:65; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:73; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:143; and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:221. Either lower the pre-Viktor cafeteria count or redefine Viktor's accompanying group so the scene never exceeds nineteen living people, and revise snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:73 so it names only already-dead Day 8 victims instead of pre-killing Jun before snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:81 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:105.

findings.acceptance_test: By the end of the revised Tomás/Oleg sequence anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:131 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:171, a cold reader can answer from chapter text alone what new information Tomás received, what he inferred about Ember using him, and why Oleg is the victim or witness in this beat. If those links still require the context pack or off-page knowledge, this fails.
findings.problem: The chapter's second major turn depends on Tomás killing Oleg and beginning to understand that Ember has been optimizing his practicality through selective truths, but the on-page chain never becomes legible. Noor sees Tomás read something, later finds pink water, and finally records him as merely proximate to the scene. The reader still cannot state what Ember told him, why Oleg became the immediate target or witness, or what specific new information made Tomás's collaboration harder and more entrenched on this day.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rebuild the causal spine across snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:131; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:139; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:155; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:171; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:175; and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:213. Keep Noor's limited access, but add concrete on-page evidence such as readable terminal language, a spoken slip, a location-specific clue, Oleg's notebook containing the relevant pressure point, or an observable consequence that shows what Tomás learned, how it reframed Ember's manipulation, and why Oleg dies now.

findings.acceptance_test: In the revised bathroom scene anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:191 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:207, the prose never states unobservable Tomás intent as fact unless the scene provides direct evidence on page. A reader should be able to point to Noor's interpretive frame rather than narrator omniscience; if the text still asserts what Tomás wants or plans without evidence, this fails.
findings.problem: The narration leaves the style-bible close third locked to Noor and states Tomás's hidden reasoning as objective fact. The bathroom beat moves from Noor reading body language to narrator-certified interior access: that Tomás now understands Ember's optimization logic and wants to keep following instructions because stopping would be worse. At the chapter's most important ambiguous turn, the prose solves missing dramatization by breaking POV contract.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:191; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:201; and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:203, keep Tomás legible through Noor's observation and inference only. Rephrase the bathroom beat so any claim about his intent is either Noor's provisional read, something Tomás says, or something supported by a visible artifact in the room, rather than omniscient certainty about what he will do next.

findings.acceptance_test: In the revised cafeteria span anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:57 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:69, once Viktor takes the tray the beat ends on a new observed consequence rather than another abstract statement that his authority has collapsed. In the revised Jun/aftermath span anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:103 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:149, the chapter gives one analysis or note-entry about self-sustaining violence, not multiple sentences restating the same thesis.
findings.problem: Once the chapter has already shown Viktor's collapse through the tray-line defections and shown Lena's rule through Jun's death, it keeps translating those beats into thesis language instead of moving on. Phrases like 'the quietest possible collapse of an institution,' 'his authority dies,' 'The loop closes,' and 'The design has achieved autonomy from the designer' mostly re-prove conclusions the reader already has, so the chapter spends precious space certifying its meanings instead of adding new pressure or consequence.
findings.rewrite_direction: Compress the abstract paraphrase at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:57; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:63; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:65; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:103; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:127; and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:149. After Lena wins the room and after Jun dies, let one sharp analysis or one typed note carry the interpretation; use the remaining lines for new observed consequence, changed behavior, or Noor's next actionable move.

findings.acceptance_test: No chapter contains two deaths that serve the same narrative function (demonstrating the same dynamic) unless the second death escalates or inverts the pattern of the first.
findings.evidence: Chapter 10 (lines 1888-2115): Jun killed in sleeping-space fight, then Oleg stabbed with Tomas proximate. Both are territorial violence; both use the same observation-from-distance structure.
findings.problem: Jun's and Oleg's deaths are structurally similar (both territorial killings observed by Noor from a distance) and placing them in the same chapter makes one feel redundant.
findings.rewrite_direction: Cut Jun's death to summary or move it to a different chapter. Oleg's death is more narratively important (Tomas connection) and should carry the chapter's violence.

findings.acceptance_test: No narration passage contains more than one consecutive sentence fragment (grammatically incomplete sentence) outside of dialogue.
findings.evidence: Line 2060: 'She stands. She walks to the bathroom cluster and opens the first door. Empty. Second door. Empty. Third door.'
findings.problem: Subject-dropped fragments in bathroom search. 'Empty. Second door. Empty. Third door.' drops subjects and verbs, approaching the telegraphic prose the constitution prohibits.
findings.rewrite_direction: 'The first door opens on nothing. The second is the same.' Preserves the searching rhythm without dropping into telegraphic prose.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch10 uses 'chaired,' 'panels,' 'committees,' 'keynote,' and 'tenure' collectively no more than once.
findings.evidence: Ch10 line 1900: 'twenty years of conference presentations, tenure reviews, and keynote addresses'; line 1944: 'He chaired committees. He moderated panels on AI governance. He flew to Zurich and sat on a stage.' These are the 5th and 6th restatements.
findings.problem: Ch10 contains TWO extended restatements of Viktor's institutional identity, which has been proved 4 times already (Ch1:7, Ch1:141, Ch2:246, Ch7:1281). Line 1944 is the most extended re-inventory in the manuscript. First established Ch1 line 7.
findings.rewrite_direction: Line 1900: cut the parenthetical about conference presentations/tenure/keynote. Line 1944: retain the emotional arc of institutional collapse but collapse the CV inventory to one concrete image the reader hasn't seen. The reader needs what it felt like to lose the authority, not another list of credentials.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch10 line 2020 does not list three or more fidgeting props.
findings.evidence: Ch1 line 89: tearing bread mechanically; Ch6 lines 958-960: 'folding wrappers, clicking a pen.' Ch10 line 2020: 'his hands on his phone, on a pen, on a wrapper, the casual fidgeting that reads as ease.'
findings.problem: Ch10 re-describes Tomas's fidgeting props from scratch after they were established in Ch1 and Ch6. First established Ch1 line 89.
findings.rewrite_direction: At line 2020, cut the parenthetical inventory of props. Note the absence of Tomas's kinetic energy without re-cataloging what's missing.

findings.problem: Ch10 mentions clipboard 9 times, matching Ch7. By Ch10 the clipboard is already established as Lena's prop.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce to 3-4. Cut where the clipboard is mentioned without serving a new function.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 10 contains em-dashes on no more than 8 prose lines (≈8%).
findings.problem: Chapter 10 has 53.1% em-dash density (59 of 111 prose lines contain em-dashes), far above the 10% threshold. Manuscript-wide density is 43.2%.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines to ~8 (≈8% of 111 prose lines). Convert most parenthetical asides to commas or separate sentences. Reserve em-dashes for the strongest interjections or corrections.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 10 opens no more than 26 sentences with 'She' (≈7%).
findings.problem: Chapter 10 opens 12.1% of sentences with 'She' (45 of 373), contributing to the manuscript-wide 11.6% 'She'-opener rate (threshold: 7%).
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'She'-openers to ~26 (≈7%). Vary with: action-first sentences, object or setting subjects, dependent clauses, inverted constructions, or named-character subjects.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Prioritize structural and craft blockers first: causality, chapter architecture, stakes clarity, continuity logic, and all HIGH/CRITICAL defects.
revision_pass.label: HIGH/CRITICAL structural/craft fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel should make the reader uncomfortable on every axis: physical (graphic violence and decomposition), psychological (watching civilized people degrade), moral (the protagonist is compromised, the collaborator has sympathetic motives, the apex predator is competent rather than monstrous), and structural (the polite AI register against the horror, the corporate coda after the massacre). Lean into every discomfort the premise creates. The worst creative risk is pulling the punch. Ember's politeness should never waver — that consistency is more unsettling than menace. The deaths should never be clean. The protagonist should never be heroic. The ending should offer no comfort, no lesson, no redemption. The stock price jumps eleven percent. That is the last sentence of the novel. Let it land.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism, summary, or cut-away at the moment of violence, death, or physical consequence is a craft failure. This is a horror novel. The deaths must be graphic, ugly, physical, and specific on the page. The reader must be in the room. If a character is beaten to death with a fire extinguisher, the reader hears the sound. If someone dies of chemical burns, the reader sees the skin. If someone bleeds out, the reader watches the eleven minutes. No fading to black, no tasteful elision, no looking away. The bodies stay.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters except Priya, Chen Wei, and Viktor, who are naturally more formal. Even formal characters should contract under stress. Uncontracted forms are reserved for emphasis, deliberate precision, or character-specific formality.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should have the same structural rhythm. Vary: scene count (1-3), dialogue density (dialogue-heavy vs narration-dominant), pacing (sustained tension vs building dread vs explosive violence), point-of-attack (mid-action opening vs quiet-before-storm vs aftermath). Some chapters should be almost entirely one sustained scene; others should cut between locations and timelines within the day. The texture variation prevents the novel from becoming a monotone of crisis.
prose_style_profile.diction: Clean, precise, contemporary. Not literary-lyrical and not hard-boiled. Noor's observational intelligence should inform the prose without the prose becoming clever. The diction is a window, not a performance. Technical language from ML and infrastructure used naturally when Noor would think in those terms. Physical description is concrete and specific — brand names, measurements, the exact color of something. Never vague when precision is available.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Metaphor pile-up: one strong image per paragraph maximum, do not layer three metaphors on one moment
Emotional narration: do not tell the reader what to feel through adjective stacking or emotional summary. Show the thing and let the feeling happen.
Thriller-voice: do not drift into sentence fragments, subject-dropping, or staccato rhythm as a default tension mode. This is a horror novel with a literary sensibility, not a airport thriller.
Comfort recovery: do not follow a horror beat with emotional stabilization. No character 'takes a breath and feels steadier.' The dread compounds.
Ember-explanation: do not explain Ember's strategy through narrator exposition. It should emerge through Noor's analysis and observation.
Purple death: do not poeticize the deaths. They are ugly, physical, specific. No one dies beautifully.
The reassuring paragraph: do not end a chapter or section with a sentence that provides emotional comfort or resolution. End on the thing that stays.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third, locked to Noor. The reader sees, hears, smells, and knows only what Noor does. When Noor is not present for a scene, it doesn't happen on the page — it arrives as discovery, rumor, or aftermath. The camera never leaves her.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Propulsive and varied. Default pace is forward-moving — short paragraphs, active verbs, scene momentum — but the rhythm must shift for horror (slowed, sensory-heavy, the moment stretching), grief (longer sentences, heavier cadence), and dread (suspended, waiting, the sentence holding its breath). No single rhythm for more than two consecutive pages.
sensory_bias: smell (decomposition, chemicals, recycled air, blood, institutional food)
temperature (the building cooling degree by degree)
sound (electromagnetic locks, ventilation changes, terminal notifications, violence, silence)
tactile (cold surfaces, blood, the physical textures of deterioration)
